CURRENT AFFAIRS,

The Belfast News Letter is still raging. The Great
Northern Railway Bill is too much for it. In the abuse
that the Irish Parliamentary Party are getting for their

~ _ advocacy for Tolerance and Fair Play in connection

with the Bigot Railway, the public can see into the truc
_inwardness of the savage howls and denunciations that
the LEADER has earned for itself from the Sourfaces
. ever since it lifted up the banner of Tolerance, Justice
and Fair - Pla,y ‘We read in the leading article in the
Belfast News Letter of July bth of “the bigoted and
nscrupulous representahvw of the Church of Rome in
she House of Commons.”” The Freeman has already
fou d out, to quote from a recent article in it, that “ It
is abominable seci;a.rmmsm on the part of Ca.thohcs to
claim fair play.”’ That is the reason that we are such
able persons in ‘the sight of the Sourfaces, the

ns and the Orangemen. Here is what the Irish

Party are gotting from the News Letter because of their
action in regard to the G. N. Railway:—

A measure may be manifestly for tho advantago of Ireland,
and entirely freo from objections, but unless the Nationalists can
make somo gain for their Church out of it they will not allow
it to pass.

And again:—

Is it any wonder that Irish Unionists, who have ten times more
interest in the country than the Nationalists, and whoso patriotism
is a reality and not o sham, are opposed to Home Rule, which
would cnable these unscrupulous bigots (o bring it to ruin?

So Irish Unionists have exactly ten times more interest
in the country than the mere low-down Nationalists,
have they? Well, anyway, in the better class jobs iu
the Great Northern Railway the “ Saved ’” have it nearly
all to themselves. And yet the. News Letter says of the
attitude of the Irish Party towards the Railway Bill: —

Nothing more unreasonable has been seen for a generation than

their attack on the Great Northern Railway Company.
Thero is plenty of stuff in the News Letter article to
stimulate the “lambs’’ on “the twelfth.”” It appears
that some of the “Idolators’’ are dreadfully energetic
persons. Here is a nice morsel for consumption, neat,
by the raw Orangeman :—

As soon as an appointment is filled in Ireland the agents of the
Roman Catholic Association inquire about the religion of the new
officer, and if he happens to be a Protestant they register the fact
in their books, to be used for the furtherance of their iniquitous
agitation for making scctarianism paramount in business, as they
have made it in education. The business capacity of the new
official does not matter; if he is not a Roman Catholic, the direc-
tors or managers who made the appointment are attacked.

But when Home Rule arrives what may happen? Listen
to this: —
They have tabulated and pigeon-holed lists of the religious pro-

fessions of employees throughout Ireland, to be used by the
priests as opportunity offers, or perhaps by a Home Rule Parlia-

ment when, following (he example of its predecessor in tho days

of James IL, it drafts a Bill of Attainder for the proscription of
Irish Protestants.

If that does not get up Archy’s blood on * the twelfth *’
nothing could. %A Bill of Attainder for the pxoscup-
tion of Irish Protestants!””  There are enough “jaw-
breakers ” in that which poor Archy, the Orangeman,
never heard tell of before to drive any Williamite
gmdow breaking and drum-beating these Orange dog

ays.

‘When Mr. Bung turns funny the sky rocks with
laughter.  There is a wit by the name of Martin
Halloran in Tralee. He runs Martin Halloran’s bar in
the Mall of that prosperous town. We think it was of
Tralee that it was said that you could not stumble in
any part of it without falling into a public-house. Mr.
Halloran issues a printed post-card, a copy of which is
before us. It is headed—" £100 Reward. Lost,
Strayed or Stolen.”” And then the wit is pumped off
as follows:—“ A man about the size of a woman, bare-
footed with a pair of wooden shoes on, pink eyes and
sunsot coloured hair, the latter cut curly and the former
cut darker. e wore a corned beef overcoat with a sour
krout lining, and had an empty sack on his back, con-
taining a barrel of sky-lights and one dozen assorted
railroad tunnels; when last seen he was following a
crowd of 50,000 people who were all trying to enter
Martin Halloran’s bar, The Mall, Tralee.”” If Martin’s
beer is anything hke his wit ono might go farther and
fare better.

The Department is not deluged exclusively with
whitewash as the inquiry proceeds. It got some tar at
Limerick and at Galway. The Very Rev. Andrew
Murphy, Adm., Vice-Chairman of the Limerick Tech-
nical Commxttee would apparently like to have said
much in favour of the technical work of the Departme:

~ s ho conscientiously could. But he had to say—" The
Department, should have formulated the scheme which
would catch the people of the country, and if that policy
‘had been adopted they should not have scen six or seven
years wasted.”’ The Department with its foreign im-
Portations based itself on text-books and theories.
Under any probable circumstances Ireland would be so
different from England that a technical schemo that
might suit England would not necessarily fit Ireland,
but the great fact about Ireland is that she is utterly
abnormal, and that is one reason why Scotchmen, how-
ever proficient they may be in the theories of  their
work, may be less than useless in Ireland. Sir Horace
Plunkett, well-intentioned though we all admit him to
be, does not know Ireland. If he did he would not have
written that foolish book for one thing.

We see that an effort is being made to get up a testi-
monial for Mr. Tim-total Harrington, who blocked the
St. Patrick’s Day Closing Bill. The “National’’
teachers are the intended victims. Surely the “National”’
teachers have enough to contend with without being
asked to subscribe “to the praiseworthy object of pre-
senting Mr. T. Harrington, M.P., with a token of the
appreciation in which he is held by the teaching body.”’

The ranks of Irish industry are poorer by the death of
Mr. John Shuley. The deceased gentleman was the
founder of the well-known Irish colour-printing firm
that bears his name, the great development of which is
a fitting monument to his business capacity and energy.
Mr. Shuley’s death was tragically sudden.  Though not
in robust health for some time past, he had been attend-
ing business as usual. Indeed, as late as Tuesday week
last our representative was speaking to him at the pre-

- mises of the firm in Stafford Street. Our representative
had called to see a fine specimen of Messrs. Shuley’s
work in the form of a pictorial poster illustrating a tcur
on the system of the Dublin, Wicklow and Wexford
Railway. The poster, which was designed by Mr. Taylor,
Manager of Messrs. Shuley and Co., and executed by
them in detail in their Dublin factory, had secured the
railway contract in face of severe foreign competition,
and the firm were naturally proud of the fact. When
our representative called to see it, he met Mr. Shuley
himself, who appeared in his ordinary health, and
whose kindly manner was the same as ever, yet within a
few hours he was to pass under the shadow of death.
We join in the widespread sympathy extended to the
relatives of Mr. Shuley in the great sorrow that has
befallen them.

A handsome address for presentation to Dr. Hyde is
: now being exhibited in the window of Messrs. R.
I Skelton, O’Connell Street. The address, which has
been the object of much admiring attention from
passers-by, was designed and painted by a member of
the Colmcille Branch of the Gaelic League. The frame
was made by Mr. Milligan, Granby Row.

It is a good thing to have street names up in Irish,
s and it is not less so when the name-plates are made in
Ireland. We have received a printed reproduction of
three name-plates erected in an Irish town. The names
of the streets appear on each in Irish and English
respectively, and the plates are manufactured by Messrs.
P. Pierce and Co., Wexford.

Why not have a special Coercion Act for Belfast. The
Lord Mayor of that town and others require looking after.
Over one hundred lodges of the Orange Secret Society
were represented af the meeting in the Ulster Hall
Belfast, when the Rev. R. D. Patterson delivered a—
" what do you think—a “sermon!”’ Amongst those pre-
~ sent were:—Mr. E. W. Penn, J.P.; R. Gibson, J.P.;
~ F. C. Johnston, J.P. ; the Lord Mayor (the Right Hon.
Sir Daniel Dixon, Bart., M.P., D.L.); Mr. G. W.
Wolff, M.P.; Mr. R. H. Reade, D.L.; Mr. Henry
Hutton, J.P.; Mr. R. H. H. Baird, J.P. According
to the “ preacher ’’ on this occasion Edward VII., the
TIdolator,”” is a perjurer. There is humour in the

The phrase “of this country *’ may puzzle some of our
friends who feel pained when we say that a large pro-
portion of people in this country are merely enemies
within the gates. The Rev. Mr. Patterson by *this
country >’ means his audiences’ country, that is, the
“United Kingdom.”” This is nice haranguing to an
audience partly composed of D.L.’s and J.P.’s.

In the presence of the Lord Mayor of Belfast and
several J.P.’s the Rev. Mr. Patterson gave a sort of
ultimatum to Edward VII. The rev. speaker said:—

But the warning should go forth to him who sat thercon that
by his conduct to the Romish party ever since he had ascended
the throne he had succeeded in exciting suspic'on, in alienating
the affection and in straining the losalty of thousands of his most
valuable subjects. (Applause). Without that loyalty and affection
Ii's throne must rock, and to regain it he must cease, and cease
al once, coquetting with the Church of Rome. (Loud applause).

After all this Orange raimeis we are not surprised to

read that the proceedings wound up with “ God Save
the King.”

“ Northern Reader,”” who recently wrote concerning
emigration from the North, replies to a question asked
him by another correspondent :— Dear Sir—I see in
your issue of the 30th ult. an inquiry about the wages
of farm labourers, and I beg to state that from £16 to
£20 a year is the common wage of a farm servant in the
North of Ireland. It may be somewhat lower in some
districts, but I have stated what the average wage is for
farm servants in County Tyrone, Armagh and Down.
Perhaps your inquirer did not understand that I
referred to farm servants, that is young men who are
capable of taking charge of horses, etc., and carrying
on the general work of the farm, as distinct from a farm
labourer who works by the day, and does not look after
the general interests of the farm. I cannot state what
is the ruling wage for other parts of Ireland. Unufor-
tunately I did not get the LEADER in time to send in
this reply earlier, but it will do in your next issue, and
in the meantime I may have a few words to say on the
emigration question.”’ P

We have before us a circular from the St. Patrick’s
Branch of the U.I.L., 127 Donegall Street, Belfast,
announcing the holding of an Qona¢ beginning on July
23rd. Thedona¢ is to be held with a view to clearing
off a furniture (we wonder was it Irish-made furniture?)
debt in connection with the branch. It is intended to
make a special display of Irish-manufactured goods. So
far, so good; bub there is no water-mark in the paper
upon which the announcement is printed. Is the paper
Irish made?

The Scotsman of July 5th contains a -con’ﬁributed 3
articlo on  Irish Nationalists and Railway Clerkships.””
The article is of the usual kind, and is intended at one

THE

same time to inflame the bigotry, and thyow
theeyes of the Big Brother. Says the writer: —
On the surface there is, of course, nothing objection-
able in an instruction to the Committee to insert a clause
providing that all appointments to clerkships consecuent
on the Bill be thrown' open to competitive examination
under rules analogous to those of the Civil Service Cora-
missioners—unless it be objected that the Legislature
;hguld leave this to the railway management. But,
unhappily, the instruction has its origin in political and
sectarian rancour. Tt is intended by the Nationalists
as a weapon which, in some way, they will be able to use
for the advantage of their party and of Catholics.” No
doul_)b it is; for it is the firm belief of Catholics and
Na.txonalis!‘:s that if the clerkships were thrown open
to competition that youths of their way of thinking
would get more places in the service of the railway than
they get on the hugger-mugger system.  The Ascend-
ancy on the Bigot Railway are afraid of open competi.
tion. The writer further states:—“It is well known
that no Protestant need apply for an appointment which
lies in the hands of public Boards controlled by Nationa-
lists.””  Of course, as our readers know, that is a
* whopper.’’

The contributor of 7'he Scotsman trots out the old
chestnut that the management repudiate the charges of
sectarianism. Then the best way to justily themselves
is to throw open the positions to competition. “Idola-
tors * are first refused entry into certain positions, and
then the boycotters turn round and taunt them with
not possessing these positions! Just as our industries
were expressly destroyed to a great extent by Act of
Parliament, and then the pious English turn up their
holy eyes at “this unhappy country.”  This writer
says:—“But it certainly appears to be true that the
proportion of Protestants on the stafis of the various
railway companies in Ireland is very greatly in excess of
that of Catholics. The natural inference is not very
complimentary to the latter.”” There you are. The
door is first slapped in your face, and then the Sourface
peeps out through the window at you, and puts his
fingers to his nose. ~ The writer goes on to say that
Gaelic—it is a wonder he did not call it Erse—is now a
leading subject in the Catholic schools, and that there
would be a demand fhat Gaelic be one of the subjects,
and that would place the Protestants at a disadvantage.
Well, Irish even as an optional subject has not yet been
placed on the programme of the G. S. and W. Railway
examinations. If Irish were placed as an optional
subject on the Great Northern Programme the Protes-
tant candidates who were not taught Irish in their
schools could take French or Latin or German instead.
The trembling Ascendancy is terrified of the spectre of
competitive examinations.

The competitions in conuection with the Leinster Tcis
will take place in Dublin on Saturday next, July 14th,
in St. Joseph’s National Schools, Dorset Street, com-
mencing ab thrée o’clock. There are twenty-two com-

purely language side of the Revival Movement. — Th>
other two are competitions in Irish history for juniors

_and seniors. The first five competitions are for essay-

~ writing and conversation. Then there are three competi-

- tions for inter-branch classes, 2nd, 3rd and 4th years

courses; one for National Schools, 3rd year’s course;

thres for Intermediate Schools, junior, mxdd}o _anrl

senior grades, and seven compet_imons in recitation,

. story-telling and oratory. There is also a specxa,l com-

 potition for the best non-native speaker of Irish over 16

M. Springborn, the proprictor of the “ Ollo’’ series
of patents, is not an Irishman. He is of German
nationality. In or about four months ago he came to Ire-
land for tho purpose of placing certain articles, of which
he is the patentee, upon the Irish market. The good§ he
camo to sell consist of patent self-acting pads for various
purposes.  They include silk and felt hat cleaning
pads, pads for black and brown boots, cloth-cleaning
pads, furniture pads, etc. Mr. Springborn was appa-
rently very successful in getting his different articles
stocked in the principal Irish wholesale houses. In the
course of his jowrney through Ireland he visited Cork.
He booked orders in Cork, and he also received a sug-
gestion.  The suggestion was an important one—
namely, that the goods which were receiving such large
Ivish support should be manufactured in Ireland. The
suggestion evidently impressed Mr. Springborn, to whom
it was given on a Wednesday. By the following Mon-
day he had taken premises at 17 Temple Street, Dublin,
and had begun to manufacture. In the meantime he
discontinued supplying orders until he was able to
announce that his productions on sale in Ireland were
made in Ireland. On Friday last some Press represen-
tatives ‘were shown through this new Dublin factory ;
over thirty girls were then employed in the different
processes of the industry. The chemical substance
enclosed in the pads will not—for the present, at any
rate—be made in Dublin. But we learn that its manu-
facture forms only a very small proportion of the total
labour engaged in making the complete article. We are
informed, for instance, that two hands could turn out
enough chemicals to keep some hundreds going at the
pad processes. It is but right to add that Irish materials
are used wherever possible in connection with this in-
dustry. The boxes which enclose the p.  are manu-
factured by O’Reilly, Poolbeg Street, Dubli. It is ad-
vauntageous for the new industry that the wo involves
a very short training, as we are informed that intel-
ligent girl will have acquired the proficiency ne sary
after a day’s instruction. In addition to supplying the
Irish demand from the Dublin factory, it is intended to
make it the producing centre for the English midlands;
and this prospect leads Mr. Springborn to believe that
the present staff in Dublin will shortly be increased six-
fold. That is a promising forecast which we trust will
be realised.

By the time those notes are published, we understand
that Mr. Springborn, who is a chemist by profession,
will have started another line of manufacture at
Temple Street. Heis the inventor and patentes of Spring-
born’s campho-oxidised bathing salt, which he believes
will be found of considerable benefit in cases of Theuma-
tism and kindred ailments. Several leading Irish dis-
tributing houses have assured him of their support for
this article, and his own confidence in it may be inferred
from the fact that he has given it his own name. We
hope that Mr. Springborn’s manufacturing enterprises
will meet with their deserved success.

business?  Will such methods help to revive Irish
industries? This man would not sell foreign-made

tion was proposed by Mr. Charles A. (?'Conn?r, K.~., a

£ TRIUMPH for Toleration * is the heading of a
b/ leading article in the Simply Deplorable.
Might we modestly suggest that “ A Triumph for the
LeADER *” would be a more appropriate heading.  So
the protracted campaign in which we embarked against
the bigotry on the Great Northern Railway has resulted
in “A Triumph for Toleration.”” It will be remem-
bered that we frequently complained, and justly com-
plained, that Mr. John Dillon, a shareholder of the
company, had never lifted even his voice on the floor of
the Board-room of the Great Northern Railway. Per-
haps Mr. Dillon was under the impression that any such
protest on his part would be as the loose phrase has it—
“ dragging religion into business.”” Be that as it may,
the matter has been boldly brought before the British
House of Commons by Mr. Jeremiah MacVeagh, and
by a vote of 161 against 31, the Bigot Railway has been
instructed by the British House of Commons to throw
open its clerkships to competitive examination.

Mr. MacVeagh presented the case admirably ; he had >
the LEADER revelations up his sleeve. The Simply
Deplorable in this matter is where it ought to be—at

ow heels. Now that we and our frienis
have done the rough work, it is wagging
its tail as it barks on the winning side. It

now says—“ It is abominable sectarianism on the part
of Catholics to claim fair play.”” Well, what about the
Provincial Bank? The state of affairs in the Provincial
Bank is far worse than on the Great Northern Rail-
way, when we take into account that a bank is a sensi-
tive institution, and it dare not defy public opinion if
public opinion is translated into action. What has the

Fair Play for Catholics who are in, or who ought to be
in, the service of that bank? We trust that at the
next general meeting of the Provincial Bank a proposal
for the establishment of open competition for clerkships
in the service of that bank will be put forward. If we had
the moulding of our own education, we probably would
head most of our youths in the direction of industrial
careers, but since we have not control, and since the
irresponsible Intermediate and “ National’’ Boards
turn education in the clerky direction, well, in the name
of all that’s sensible let us fight for all the clerkships
that are going. The Christian Brothers are turning out
boys every year that could probably fill all the clerk-
ships in the country. y

FURDAMENTAL TRUTHS.*
HE Most Rev. Dr. O’Dea said some time ago
that the dangers of the Catholic Church of the
future will be ‘‘intellectual dangers.”” Persecution in
the sense of the’ Penal days is dead. Even the per-
secution in progress now in France is not the persecu-
tion of the days of old.  Religion is being gradually
starved out, but not by means of tortures, and other
physical agencies. We, here in Ireland, have not yet
felt the pressure of the peculiar dangers to our Faith,
characteristic of the present age. But we shall soon,
and there is no use in closing our eyes to the fact.
Scientific difficulties are cropping up that seem to con-
flict with the teachings of Religion.
nected with the Biblical criticism are in the air, even
across the channel.

The publications of the Rationalistic Press are being
sold publicly in our cities. What provision is being
made to meet all these dangers to our Faith? Practi-
cally nothing is being done. In contrast to England,
Germany, Belgium, America, practically nothing is
being done in Ireland in face of real dangers to Catholi-
cism. The Dogmatic publications of the Catholic
Truth Society in England, and the Westminster Lec-
tures, are a specimen of what Catholics of England are
doing. Similar means are had recourse to in Germany,
America, etc. But we are resting with folded arms in
TIreland, and trusting to the prayers of St. Patrick to
keep us all right. The prayers of St. Patrick and the
other saints of Erin will save us if we are true to our-
selves. But if we throw away deliberately our most
precious gift—our Faith—not even the Almighty Him-
self can save us.

Now what special provision should be made to guard,
especially the younger generation, against the peculiar
dangers of our time? There is but one provision, and
that is the establishment throughout the country of
systematic religious teaching. - Many may smile at this
being the solution. They say: ‘‘ Have we not got it’
already’”” ?* ““Is not the catechism taught to the chil-
dren in the schools daily ”’? ‘“ Does not the priest do
it on Sunday in the church ’’? We admit that it is so.
But granting that this is being done, even in the most
efficient, style—about which, however, there might be
some difference of opinion—what about the boys who
have left school? What is being done for them during
that most critical period between boyhood and man-
hood—the time when they are beginning to think for
hemselves, the period of most intellectual and moral

they are in the colleges and seminaries and Christian
Brothers’ schools, they do get some religious instruc-
tion, I grant, especially in the Chrxst_mn Brothers’
schools.  But even here so much attention is paid to
sccular instruction, and public examinations are so im-
portant that it is gradually crushed out, and becomes
perfunctory. It is of second best importance ; that is
all. Then, what about the vast majority of boys who *
are not in the colleges, etc.; what is done for them =
after their Confirmation and First Communion? In
most cases they never again get any systematic reli-
gious instruction. They may hear sermons at last
Mass on Sundays, if they go to it. But very often they
don’t go to it. They dodge the Mass with the sermon.
Lven if they do go, they only pick up bits and scraps
of instruction. They may hear sermons during a Mis-
sion. Bub these sermons are mostly moral ones, not
dogmatic. They are roused up to the good if they
have fallen into sin. But all religious teaching passing
away with the Mission, they fall back into the old rut,
and so it continues.

Like everything else in Ireland, the Religious life of
the country is in an abnormal state; and there is great
fear when the real intellectual dangers that Dr. O’Dea
speaks of, come on, the country will not be able to cope
with them. There are at present among our young
men, many who have lost their Catholic spirit, are
anti-clerical, and even non-practical Catholics, and all
hecause they have never been systematically grounded
in true Catholic principles. Should infidel principles
even get a foothold in this country, these young fellows
will be their first votaries.  Should these young men
emigrate, and get iuto an alien and hostile atmosphere,
then good-bye to their faith.

These thoughts have been suggested by the publica-
tion of a very remarkable little book by Father Bodkin;
of Castleknock College, ““The Great Fundamental
Truths of Religion,”” which is designed to help in this
very matter of systematic Religious Instruction, and to
help the very class we are speaking of—young Catholics
in the higher classes in Colleges and Universities, and
those who have left school. 1t goes on the right lines
emphatically. It takes up only fundamentals; but it
insists on these. It does not expect every Catholic
young man to be a trained theologian, but it requires
that every Catholic can, and should give an account of
the Faith that is in him; that he should give it boldly,
not, apologetically.  There is nothing to be ashamed of,
or to apologise tor, in being a Catholic; on the con-
trary, there is everything to be proud of, and glory in.
The Catholic system is the most perfect, the most
rational of religious systems, yet the most simple. It is
suited to the learned and the ignorant. Any Catholic
who lives according to the teachings of his Faith, lives
an ennobled life, and adopts the best means to succeed,
both in this life and the next.

Passing over accidentals, Father Bodkin takes the
essentials of Catholic belief. e takes the Existence of
God the Creator and Lord of the Universe. He takes
God’s object in creating man, and what man must do
to bo saved. Father Bodkin sums it up thus:—DMan
must have Faith, and live according to its teachings.
He then takes up Faith, and the need for an infallible
guide in such an important matter. Great stress is
laid on the doctrine of the Divinity of Our Lord, and
that the Catholic Church is the sure depository of Fis
teaching to the end of time. A great deal of space is
given a_lsu' to difficulties against Faith, and the very
best principles are laid down for answering them. It
is a hook every Catholic ought to have, especially
young Catholics. We trust it will be taken up by the
schools and Colleges for their higher classes. It is
especially the book for the present age.

It is to be hoped that the whole question of serious
religious teaching, in accordance with the wishes of
our present Holy Father, will be taken up by our
Catholic people, priest, and laymen alike. There should
be a revival here, too, in methods at least better adapted
to the present age. The Irish Ireland spirit, shoulc
have its influence on the Church, just as the Church
its turn should bring its ennobling influence to bear
the Irish Ireland movement. Thus in the new

;:[AT of the Inclustri·al Revival in Belfast 1 One 
firm south of the Boyne told a Belfast customer 
that their goods were not stocked by any of the Belfast 
houses. 
'Dhey added-" The fact is, we have never sent 
our representative with samples .to Belfast, as ,we are of 
opin-ion it would be loss of time." Whether this indi-
cates a lack of push on tho part of the firm, or whether 
it is the result of a well-grounded belief that Belfast 
is a poor market for Irish goods, we are ?ot in a position 
to offer an opinion. But are Belfast Irish Irelanclers so 
insignificant a body that they are not worth troubling 
about 1 To go no further, the 1rnrchasing power of _our 
readers in Belfast is a very considerable thing, a thi~g, 
like Ireland, worth fighting for. 
No doubt ,the Irish 
Irelanders of Belfast are only a small proportion. of the 
population, for w-hen you subtract the Orangem~n, ~ng-
land's Faithful 
Garrison 
and those 
Nationalists, 
who do not bother about Irish industry .or :the Irish lan-
guage, there are only a very small minority left. ~ut 
surely these could talk to their shopkeepers. 
If our 
readers up there spoke to their shopkeeper~,. a_nd 
demanded goods of Irish manufacture, and, fa1hng to 
get them, demanded the reason why, they would quickly 
make a marked impr.ession on t he shop world of Belfast. 
Incidentally the answers, excuses, arguments. of the 
various shopkeepers who failed to supply Insh-macle 
goods would be interesting information if such of our 
readers who found it convenient to <lo so sent us on 
particulars. 
A Belfast correspondent, in the cours~ of 
a letter, writes:-" True, we can get only very httle 
Irish manufacture in "Belfast, but if only people would 
ask for Irish, and try several shops before finally pur-
chasing, it would have a good effect. Shopkee:pers are 
in the busine~ to make money, not for • se-nt1mental 
reasons; so the only way .to influence th~m . is to. let 
them see they are losing money by not stockmg Insh-
rnade goods. ' ' 
Our Belfast readers will see at once that if they are 
good enough t o go to some trouble-and going to trouble 
is a great personal sacrifice-they could do a consider-
able amount of work for the furtherance of Irish manu-
facture. 
One customer who speaks up to his shop-
keeper can, in most cases, induce or compel him to stock 
a certain line of hish goods. 
That means that the 
shopkee·per has been induood to open an accoun_t with a 
pa-rticular Irish manufacturer, and from the opening of 
t hrut account anything may follo-w. 
A customer who 
iuduooo a shopkeeper to open an account with an Irish 
mam1facturer may lay the flattering unction to his soul 
that he has clone a big day's work for Irish incfostry. 
To <lo .this entails self-sacrifice, though no monetary 
sacrifice. It is disagreeable for the ordinary, amiable 
person to ta-ke up an a,ttitucle of-" supply me with this; 
tell me why you won't supply it, a.n<l if you ought to 
supply it, and don't, l shall go to a-nother shop." 
That 
requires volition, and the exercise of one's will-power in 
the cause of Irish industry is a species of self-sacrifice 
which w~, without any ;:eservation, would ask our 
readers to make on every possible occasion. 
As far as 
we are concerned, anyone who chooses '.;o do so may give 
more money for a thing because it is Irish if he chooses; 
but we base the claims of Irish goods on the general 
principle of " as good and as cheap." If they cannot 
stand competition, there is little or no iife in them, and 
no future before them. 
But our readers who are 
anxious to do something in the self-sacrificing way for 
Irish industry should lose no opportunity of speaking 
to their shopkeepers, and, if necessary, bringing pres-
sure to bear upon them with a view to inducing them to 
stock Irish goods. A great deal has been clone here 
and .there through Ireland, but Belfast would seem to 
offer a big and fruitful field for the Irish industrial

WHAT’S TQ BE DONE WITH TRINITY ?

HE Trinity College Commission has sent round
circulars asking for views as to what steps should
be taken to extend the usefulness of that foundation,
and one of these documents has come my way. As I
have not the leisure or the presumption to offer any
statement of view to that august tribunal, I shall instead
set forth such thoughts as occur to me in these columns.
Perhaps, indeed, expressions of opinion are more likely
to reach the public eye in the pages of this journal than
if they figure among the ““also ran’’ in the back pages
of a massive report. I trust, however, that as to such wit-
nesses as the Commission do examine, they will have
their proceedings open to the press, and will not, as on
the last occasion, kill all interest in their sittings by
secrecy, and then some fine morning publish the evidence
of all the witnesses overwhelmed by each other’s
verbosity.

Possibly a brief summary of the facts will most
clearly enable us to understand the issues. Leaving out
students studying for the Protestant Church, who may
be set off by Maynooth, there are some seven or eight
hundred students attending lectures at Dublin Univer-
sity, and a number of lay students only slightly less
attend the Catholic University, if by a broad construc-
tion we include the associated ladies’ colleges under that
term. A body of students, about half either of these in
numbers, attend lectures at Queen’s College, Belfast—
that is to say roughly:—

or more of them, our views must necessarily take a defi-
nite shape. We see at once that even supposing reli-
gion out of the question altogether, we must either satis-
factorily provide for the separate existence of the three
colleges, or else we must amalgamate them upon reason-
able terms. You must have either amalgamation or
autonomy. There are a number of persons in the
country who favour the policy of having only one Uni-
versity, and who wish that Catholics had entered
Trinity in 1874. But it is a strange thing that few of
these are ready to face the logical outcome of their
opinion, If Catholics had entered Trinity at that date
in large numbers, and been put at no disadvantage, about
half the Fellows of Trinity would now be the gentlemen
who are Fellows of the Royal University; a glance ab
their respective achievements in examination will show
this at once. And if you want to put things as nearly
as possible in the same position as if Catholics had
entered in 1874, you can only do so by amalgamating
Trinity with the Catholic University, and by appointing
forty or fifty Catholic University Professors to be
Fellows and Professors of Trinity College on an equal
footing with those already there.

Yet I venture to say that this policy is the one least
thought of by those who desire “'to bring together the
youth of Ireland in one common University.”” The
mere suggestion of such an intrusion is thought to be
insane presumption. Is an ancient academic founda-
tion like Trinity to admit a body of intruders? The pro-
posal is absurd. But that is just what it must do if it
is to claim to educate the youth ef Ireland. If it wants
the students it must take the professors also; that is
both logic and reason. If it were proposed to relieve
the financial necessities of Cambridge University by
amalgamating it with the wealthier Oxford—by the way,
no one has proposed this most simple expedient—would
it be suggested that the proper course was to turn the
Cambridge Professors off upon pension or upon the way-
side, and permit the Cambridge students to enter upon
equal terms for the Oxford matriculation? What an
outery would go up from the scientific world when it was
proposed that Professor Thompson, for instance, be
silenced for the rest of his days. Yet it is almost in
terms the suggestion put forward by most of the “one-
college *’ advocates in Ireland. For Trinity is very ready
for a Catholic capitulation, but by no means ready for a
peace upon equal terms.

Now, personally I do not favour an amalgamation
policy.  The bishops have, indeed, never actually ex-
pressed approval of a system somewhat like that of Bonn
University, where there would be, on the whole, a bal-
ance bhetween the Catholic and Protestant element in
the professorate. To use a formula adopted before, they
have hitherto agreed to C,, Py, Cy P, or even Cg P3,
but they have never formally accepted the mixture,
C; P, or C; Py, a German concoction, though with
proper safe-guards, even that might not be prejudicial.
Again I do not think that the policy of a common Uni-
versity is the best from an educational stand-point. But
as far as I know, it is not from Catholics or Catholic
bishops that the real objection will come to amalgama-
tion on equal terms. Trinity and the Episcopalian Pro-
testants will fight against it tooth and nail. They pro-
fess to approve the decoction C, P, as a beverage for
posterity, but they desire that the “ C’’ should lie in
bond for fifty years in a Trinity cask, and they very much
dislike raw “C,”’ except in minute quantities, to be
added to the silent spirit.  Amalgamation is a just
policy in that it gives no advantage to either side, but:
on the other hand, it is, if it Be honestly carried ou,
a policy even more distasteful to Proteslants than to
Catholics, and even more unsatisfactory to educationa-
lists than to those interested from a religious point of
view. Amalgamation—honest amalgamation on equal
terms—is the only way in which justice could be secured
by a mere change in the constitution of Trinity College,
and that is a policy which neither side really want.

There remains, then, the only alternative—the
autonomy of separate colleges; and here you have the
Dunraven scheme of a college in Dublin University, the
Commission scheme of a college in the Royal University,

and the scheme of a separate University for Catholics.
In reality, the three are but one . scheme,
for an autonomous college, if it really be
autonomous, is but another name for a Univer-
sity, whilst the suggestion that under the Dunraven
schemo the Catholic Nationalists from tho Stephen’s
Green College would associate with the Protestant
Unionists from Trinity is utterly absurd. More-
over, under this, as under the  youth-of-Ireland-
in-common ’’ scheme, the Presbyterians, the body,
heaven knows, most in need of such emolliating in
fluences as are proposed to be brought to bear, are still
to be left to ferment in the denominational solitude of
Belfast.  And as for the Burke cum Grattan ancestral
glories to the heirship of which the Catholic College in
Dublin University would succeed, as compared with the
humbler lustre of the Royal, which can boast of nothing
famous except an organ, I believe that an equal degree
of unearned prestige could be secured in a far more
simple manner by conferring a peerage on the Presi-
dent and knighting the Professors.

As between these schemes, if honestly carried out, I
have never secen much difference. Educationally, they
are the same schemes, which are disguised in different
costumes, to suit political expediency. I prefer, indeed,
the independent University rather than the autonomous
colleges, because it is the only honest scheme of the
three, and sails under its own colours. But I do not
think Catholics are in general much concerned about
what flag the vessel hoists, provided it is not filled with
a hostile crew. We demand equality of treatment, and
our only preference is that we receive that equality by
a policy of autonomy which is satisfactorily and educa-
tionally sound rather than by one of amalgamation,
which, though it be just, is yet one that no one has ever
offered, no one desires, and no one approves of.

AN IRISH AMERICAN ON “TAME” CATHOLICS.

RISH-AMERICANS all over this large Continent
read, with much pleasure, THE LEADER. How
refreshing are its denunciations of bigotry and
the so-called backboneless patriots, as well as
its  enthusiastic advocacy of Irish  industry,
temperance and  self-reliance, and  everything
that tends to make a nation. For the above reasons, I
send you the following facts which I noticed while in
Ireland last year:—It was the unity that existed all
over Ireland among the Orange, Freemason and kindred
societies, and the total want of organisation in Catholic
bodies, except in passing resolutions and other windy
mouthings. They are so nice that many of them will
tell you that it might offend their Protestant brethren
if they had a Catholic defence association, and poor
weaklings that they are, will let such institutions as Bel-
~fast Corporation, banks, railways and business houses
(who actually live by them) discriminate and deny them
positions simply on account of their religion. Edmund
Burke’s saying—‘ It was harder to make a freeman of
a slave than a slave of a freeman ’—is exemplified in
them ; the yoke of slavery or the ascendancy party is not
yet removed from their minds. Every decent Protes-
tant as well as Catholic likes fair play, and no Protes-
tant will fall out with a Catholic if he sticks up for his
rights; and, believe me, this will never he got in Ireland
unless through unity.  How, before Parnell’s time,
Dublin and every other part of Ireland was so generous
that they would almost give preference to a Protestant
to prove their liberality, and look at the return Belfast
and many other places give. Don’t, for a moment, think
that I am a bigot, far be it from me; any Christian
cannot, be a bigot; he believes everyone has a right to
worship as he thinks fit, and does not belicve that thoe
worshipping at one place or other should disqualify
him from earning a livelihood ; and why should that be

As is stated in the last article, no such document as

are mentioned in the last article. This paper, or docu-
ment, is not dated; but its subject matter has fixed its
date to have been between 1723 and 1762, and most
probably immediately before the latter date. For we
may be certain that the Board of Trinity College lost
no time in accepting such an offer. The document is

styled : —

of

the document, in which we read:—“The present
encouragement is, small salaries to Mathematics and
Hebrew Lecturers. Thirty pounds a year to each. The
latter is paid by this Board.’”  The Erasmus Smith

A Srrareur QuestioN To Sir C. NIXON, BART.
Dublin, July 7th, 1906.

Sir—1In this week’s issue you say: ‘ Sir Chnsbophgr
Nixon, who sent his son to Trinity, also orated. Sir
Christopher said kind words of the Senate of the Royal -
University. Ile said:—‘I can truly say after an ex-
perience of nearly twenty years as a member of the
Senate, I do not remember any claim presented, with
justice and reason, which my colleagues on the Senate
who differ from me, in religion as well as in other con-
siderations, have not always been most anxious to do
all they could to show their sympathy with their
penalised brethren.””” Now Professor Antony Roche
is one of the penalised brethren of Sir Christopher’s
colleagues.  In deference to the wishes of Cardinal
Cullen he avoided Trinity College, and so found him-
self bare of any academic honour after a quarter of w
century’s strenuous scientific work at the Cecilia St.
School of Medicine. Even Convocation of the Royal
University recommended him for an honorary degree,
and it was thought that the Senate would be delighted
to follow the recommendation. But Professor Roche
has not got his honorary yet, and when, as a member
of the Annual Committee of Convocation, I inquired
why its wishes had not been acceded to more promptly,
it was hinted to me that Sir Christopher Nixon, who
ropresented the Cecilia Street School on the Senate of the
Royal University, was not hotly in favour of giving
his ‘‘ penalised colleague’’ the degree. Of course, Sir
Christopher Nixon may be able to refute absolutely the
suggestion, but some of us are anxious to find out who
is really responsible.—Yours truly,

J. C. M‘WALTER.
